Analyzing Average Fruit Prices of 2023

Anna Weiss, Lauren Moylan, Anthony Ferraro

3/29/2026

Introduction

Description of the Data

Our data set is from the US Department of Agriculture, focusing on the average national cost of various fruits in 2023. For each fruit, the data set measures the form of fruit (canned, fresh, dried, etc.), the average retail price of each fruit, the unit of measure (per pound, per pint, etc.), the preparation yield factor, size of a cup equivalent of each fruit, the size of a cup equivalent with each unit of measure (pounds, pints, etc.), and the average price per a cup equivalent (i.e. how much a cup of a fruit would cost). https://www.ers.usda.gov/data-products/fruit-and-vegetable-prices

Data Import

The data set was already neat and tidy when we acquired it. We were able to download the CSV file straight from the government website.

fruit_prices_2023 <- read_csv("fruit-prices-2023.csv")
## Rows: 62 Columns: 8
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## chr (4): Fruit, Form, AverageRetailPriceUnitOfMeasure, CupEquivalentUnitOfMe...
## dbl (4): AverageRetailPrice, PreparationYieldFactor, SizeOfACupEquivalent, A...
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.

Univariate Data

Form of Fruit

ggplot(fruit_prices_2023, aes(x = fct_infreq(Form), fill = Form)) +
  geom_bar() +
  labs(title = "Count of Each Fruit Form", x = "Form of Fruit") +
  theme_bw()

fruit_prices_2023 %>%
  count(Form) %>%
  arrange(desc(n)) %>%
  knitr::kable()
Form n
Fresh 24
Canned 12
Juice 11
Dried 9
Frozen 6

From the bar plot, we can see that Fresh is the most popular form of fruit to be sold in and Frozen is the least popular form of fruit to be sold in. In our numercial summary table, we can see that of the 62 fruits, 24 of them are sold as Fresh, 12 sold as Canned, 11 sold as Juice, 9 sold Dried, and, last but not least, 6 sold Frozen.

Average Retail Price

ggplot(fruit_prices_2023, aes(x = AverageRetailPrice)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.9) +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Fruit for each Average Retail Price", x = 
         "Average Retail Price")

favstats(~AverageRetailPrice, data = fruit_prices_2023) %>%
  knitr::kable()
min Q1 median Q3 max mean sd n missing
0.4058254 1.619546 2.521503 4.296073 10.10969 3.317489 2.315873 62 0

From the histogram, we can see that that the most common average retail price for a fruit is around 2.50 dollars. Seldom does the average price of a fruit exceed 5.00 dollars. From our number summary, we can see that the minimum average retail price for a fruit is 0.41 dollars and the maximum average retail price for a fruit is 10.11 dollars.

Size of a Cup Equivalent

ggplot(fruit_prices_2023, aes(x = SizeOfACupEquivalent)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.05) +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Fruit for Proportion of a Cup", x = "Proportion 
       of a Cup")

favstats(~SizeOfACupEquivalent, data = fruit_prices_2023) %>%
  knitr::kable()
min Q1 median Q3 max mean sd n missing
0.1232 0.32245 0.3638 0.5 0.5401 0.3743387 0.1201763 62 0

From the histogram, we can see that the most common proportion of a cup for a fruit in the data set to be is around 0.36. From the number summary, we can see that the minimum proportion of cup a fruit in the data set is 0.1232 and the maximum is 0.5401.

Bivariate Data

Average Retail Price by Form

ggplot(fruit_prices_2023, aes(x = AverageRetailPrice, color = Form, fill = 
                                Form)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(title = "Average Retail Price by Form", x = "Average Retail Price")

favstats(AverageRetailPrice ~Form, data = fruit_prices_2023) %>%
  knitr::kable()
Form min Q1 median Q3 max mean sd n missing
Canned 1.2080994 2.037711 2.314850 2.504131 6.308314 2.503112 1.2645912 12 0
Dried 4.3283440 5.887861 7.947967 8.174637 10.109685 7.255790 1.8210085 9 0
Fresh 0.4058254 1.373210 2.082588 2.895714 7.966755 2.513090 1.8814364 24 0
Frozen 3.4675070 3.869653 4.323840 4.641475 6.209611 4.465068 0.9659068 6 0
Juice 0.9511151 1.187125 1.794818 3.113238 3.401423 2.112754 1.0006342 11 0

From the density plot, we can see that the average retail price for canned fruit peaks at around 2.4 dollars, meaning it is the most common average retail price for canned fruit. Fresh and dried fruit have the widest distributions of price, with dried fruit tending to be the most expensive. From the number summary, canned fruit and juiced fruit have the lowest average price among the forms, with canned fruit costing an average of 2.50 dollars and juiced fruit costing an average of 2.11 dollars. Dried fruit is the most expensive by far with an average cost of 7.26 dollars.

Comparing Average Retail Price and Size of a Cup Equivalent

ggplot(fruit_prices_2023, aes(x = AverageRetailPrice, y = SizeOfACupEquivalent)) + 
  geom_point(color = "maroon") +
  geom_smooth(color = "hotpink") +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(title = "Comparing the Average Retail Price to the Size of a Cup 
       Equivalent of Each Fruit",  x = "Average Retail Price", y = "Size of a 
       Cup Equivalent")
## `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'

cor(fruit_prices_2023$AverageRetailPrice, fruit_prices_2023$SizeOfACupEquivalent) %>%
  knitr::kable()
x
-0.6721787

From the graph, we can see that as the size of a fruit goes down, the cost of it goes up. From our correlation command, we can see that there is a moderately strong negative relationship between average retail price and sie of a cup equivalent of each fruit, r = -0.67.

Trivariate Data

Comparing Fruit Form, Average Retail Price, and Size of a Cup Equivalent: Dot Plot

ggplot(fruit_prices_2023,aes(x = AverageRetailPrice, y = SizeOfACupEquivalent))+
  geom_point(aes(color = Form)) +
  geom_smooth() +
  labs(title = "Comparing Average Price and Size of Fruit by Form", 
       x = "Average Retail Price", y = "Size of A Cup Equivalent") +
  theme_bw() 
## `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'

Including what we found out from our previous graph, we can see that canned fruit tends to be the largest proportionally to a cup and the cheapest, whereas dried fruit tends to be the smallest proportionally to cup and the most expensive.

Comparing Fruit Form, Average Retail Price, and Size of a Cup Equivalent:

Fasceted

ggplot(fruit_prices_2023,aes(x = AverageRetailPrice, y = SizeOfACupEquivalent)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = Form)) +
  facet_wrap(~Form) +
  theme_bw()

fruit_prices_2023 %>%
  group_by(Form) %>%
  summarize(correlation = cor(AverageRetailPrice, SizeOfACupEquivalent, 
                              method = "pearson", use = "complete.obs")) %>%
  knitr::kable()
## Warning: There were 2 warnings in `summarize()`.
## The first warning was:
## ℹ In argument: `correlation = cor(...)`.
## ℹ In group 4: `Form = "Frozen"`.
## Caused by warning in `stats::cor()`:
## ! the standard deviation is zero
## ℹ Run `dplyr::last_dplyr_warnings()` to see the 1 remaining warning.
Form correlation
Canned -0.3462057
Dried -0.2872053
Fresh -0.2843730
Frozen NA
Juice NA

Now that the data is separated by fruit form, we can see that fresh fruit has the widest range of both average retail price and size proportional to a cup. Both frozen fruit and juiced fruit have really smile ranges in terms of size. Consistent with previous plots, canned fruit and fruit juice are typically the cheapest forms of fruit and dried fruit is typically the most expensive. From our number summary, we can see that each from of fruit has a moderately weak correlation with both size and average price.

Conclusion

Based on the multiple graphs looking at each variable individually as well as together, there are many correlations between the variables. First, in many of the graphs it is visible that dried fruit is on average the most expensive fruit while fresh fruit tends to have the largest range of retail price. On the other end, canned fruit and juiced fruit tends to have the cheapest average retail price. Looking at equivalent of a cup for the various fruits, both canned fruit and juiced fruit tend to be sold in the same size proportional to a cup. Dried fruit tends to be the smallest of the forms and frozen fruit falls right in the middle. So to with the average price, fresh fruit has the widest range in size proportional to a cup.
